document.title: M1 Lattice: Merging Guide
document.purpose: Provides guidance for combining multiple M1 transport units within applications. Merging behavior is not prescribed by the M1 specification and is left to the discretion of the consuming system.

core_statement: Merging behavior is defined by the consuming application. M1 does not prescribe how data should be combined, prioritized, or resolved.

merging_authority.description: Applications are responsible for determining how M1 data is merged and interpreted.
principles: Applications determine which data to import, retain, or discard.
Applications may prioritize sources, timestamps, or other criteria.
No single transport unit is authoritative.

merging_model.description: Merging is the process of combining contributions from multiple transport units into a working set.
principles: Merging combines contributions; it does not resolve truth.
All data may be preserved unless explicitly discarded by the application.
Conflicts should not be silently resolved without explicit policy.
Merging behavior should be consistent and predictable within an application.

merging_process.description: A common baseline approach for merging transport units.
steps: Initialize an empty mapping of entity-id to aspects.
For each entity-id in the unit:
If the entity-id is not present, create a new entry.
For each aspect associated with the entity:
If the aspect-id is not present, attach it.
If the aspect-id is already present:
If the aspect content is identical, do nothing.
If the aspect content differs, treat as a conflict.
notes: This process is illustrative, not prescriptive.

conflict_handling.description: Conflicts occur when multiple values exist for the same entity and aspect-id.
approaches: Accumulate: Preserve all values and defer interpretation.
Select: Choose a single value based on defined criteria.
Annotate: Record conflicts explicitly as data.
Partition: Maintain separate views or layers per source.
notes: No default conflict resolution strategy is defined by M1.
Applications are responsible for defining their own approach.

guidance.description: Considerations to assist implementers in designing merging behavior.
questions: What should happen when two aspects of the same type differ?
Should all values be preserved, or should one be selected?
If selecting one value, what determines priority (e.g., source, timestamp, trust level)?
How should identical values be detected and deduplicated?
Should conflicting data be surfaced to the user?
Should conflicts be recorded as data (e.g., in logs or aspects)?
Are some aspects additive (e.g., logs), while others are singular (e.g., titles)?
Should merging be deterministic across runs?

final_notes: Different applications may implement different strategies for the same data.
The goal of this guide is to support thoughtful and explicit design decisions.
